data.title: U.S. grants 30-day waiver for purchases of Russian oil stranded at sea
data.description:
The United States has issued a 30-day waiver allowing countries to purchase sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products already stranded at sea, expanding a temporary exemption granted last week to India alone.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a post on X that this was a “narrowly tailored, short-term measure” that applies only to oil already in transit.

The move aims to ease pressure on global oil prices as the war in the Middle East continues.

The waiver makes Russian crude and fuel aboard about 30 tankers in Asian waters potentially available for purchase, provided the cargoes were already at sea when the exemption was granted.

According to ship-tracking data compiled by Bloomberg, the vessels are carrying at least 19M barrels of Russian crude and about 310,000 tons of refined products. Most of the refined cargo consists of naphtha, used in plastics production, along with some diesel, both of which have surged in price since Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz.

About 25 vessels are transporting crude, including grades such as Sokol on ships near China. Several tankers in the Arabian Sea are also loaded with Russia’s medium-sour Urals blend.

Many of the ships are signaling “for orders,” indicating they have no confirmed destination, while others list Singapore or Malaysia, which are common waiting points where tankers hold cargo while buyers are sought.

The U.S. has taken several steps to curb surging crude and fuel prices since strikes on Iran began nearly two weeks ago. These include plans to release 172M barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. Officials have also floated additional measures, ranging from intervening in futures markets to suspending a century-old law that requires US-flagged ships to transport goods between American ports.

Still, Brent crude (CO1:COM) is trading over $100 a barrel on Friday, on track for a 9% weekly surge. Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) traded at $96.7 a barrel, on track for a 6% weekly surge.

data.title: Nektar outlines $400M–$460M year-end 2026 cash target as REZPEG enters Phase III in atopic dermatitis
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Howard W. Robin described 2025 as a pivotal year, highlighting "the first positive results from the 2 Phase IIb studies of REZPEG in atopic dermatitis and alopecia areata," validating the company's Treg mechanism for clinically meaningful outcomes in inflammatory disease. Robin noted, "Monthly or quarterly dosing of REZPEG during the maintenance period showed a deepening of response with increases in EASI-75, EASI-90 and an up to fivefold increase in EASI-100 scores." He emphasized the narrowing competitive landscape, stating, "We believe this puts REZPEG in a lead position as a novel MOA."
  • Robin announced, "We ended 2025 with $245.8 million in cash and investments and with no debt on our balance sheet. Since year-end, we've also raised approximately $476 million in additional net cash." He signaled readiness to move into Phase III for REZPEG in atopic dermatitis, with the first patient to be randomized in June and "alignment with the FDA on our Phase III dose, the 24-week induction treatment period and other critical Phase III study design elements."
  • CFO Sandra Gardiner reported, "Our revenue was $21.8 million for the fourth quarter and $55.2 million for the full year 2025... Our net loss for the fourth quarter was $36.1 million or $1.78 basic and diluted net loss per share. For the full year of 2025, our net loss was $164.1 million or $9.73 basic and diluted net loss per share."

Outlook

  • Gardiner provided preliminary 2026 guidance: "Our noncash royalty revenue for the full year of 2026 is expected to be between $40 million and $45 million." R&D expenses are forecasted at "between $200 million and $250 million," G&A expense is expected to decline to "$60 million to $65 million," and "full year noncash interest expense is expected to be between $30 million and $35 million."
  • Gardiner stated, "We expect to end 2026 with approximately $400 million to $460 million in cash and investments."
  • Management reiterated plans to update 2026 financial guidance after completing Phase III budgeting.

Financial Results

  • Gardiner reported, "Our R&D expenses were $29.7 million for the fourth quarter and $117.3 million for the full year. Our G&A expenses were $11.2 million for the fourth quarter and $68.7 million for the full year."
  • Noncash interest expense for the fourth quarter was stated as $9.8 million and $26.2 million for the full year.
  • The company completed a public offering for $460 million, resulting in approximately $432 million in net cash proceeds, and accessed about $44 million from an ATM facility in 2026.

Q&A

  • Yasmeen Rahimi, Piper Sandler: Asked about new data for the REZOLVE-AA study at AAD and competitive benchmarks for the 16-week extension. Jonathan Zalevsky responded that data will show "the additional effect of treatment with REZPEG and the potential of additional patients to convert to achieving SALT 20 responses, as well as deepening their SALT reductions."
  • Julian Harrison, BTIG: Inquired about ACQ5 data inclusion for Phase III and patient cohorts. Mary Tagliaferri confirmed, "We are including the ACQ5 in the Phase III program... we will make every effort to include that into the label," with approximately 25% bioexperienced patients and 75% naive.
  • Cheng Li, Oppenheimer: Asked about REZPEG formulation and mitigation in Phase III. Zalevsky explained plans for commercial auto-injector launch and weight-banded dosing, while Tagliaferri described ISRs as "99% mild to moderate in severity" and rare dropouts.
  • Jiale Song, Jefferies: Queried efficacy benchmarks compared to Olumiant. Zalevsky reiterated the study remains blinded but emphasized REZPEG's aim to match low-dose JAK efficacy with a differentiated safety profile.
  • Mayank Mamtani, B. Riley: Asked about off-therapy data and EASI-100 as a differentiator. Robin highlighted, "EASI-100 hasn't been looked at very closely... very few people attain the levels that we've been able to attain."
  • Samantha Semenkow, Citi: Asked about the type 1 diabetes study and indication prioritization. Zalevsky and Tagliaferri described the TrialNet study design and projected C-peptide as a key endpoint, noting an outpatient regimen for REZPEG compared to IV-based TZIELD.
  • Yu He, H.C. Wainwright: Inquired about extension phase completion and litigation with Eli Lilly. Zalevsky confirmed "23 patients were ongoing in the 16-week extension" and all will stop treatment at week 36 or 52, followed by a 24-week off-drug period. Robin said the Lilly trial is scheduled for September 8.
  • Tsan-Yu Hsieh, William Blair: Asked about market sizing and competition. Zalevsky and Robin underscored the large atopic dermatitis market and REZPEG's differentiated MOA; Tagliaferri compared efficacy favorably to nemolizumab plus topical corticosteroids.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on competitive differentiation, trial design, safety, market potential, and upcoming data catalysts, often pressing for clarity on data presentation and commercial positioning. The tone was slightly positive, with questions targeting validation of efficacy and differentiation from existing therapies.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone throughout, emphasizing the strength of clinical data, robust cash position, and readiness for Phase III. Robin and Zalevsky consistently referenced "differentiated safety profile" and "deepening of response," supporting confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady, while analysts' questions shifted more toward upcoming data catalysts and commercialization strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter revealed significant cash inflow from financing activities, compared to a smaller increase in the prior quarter.
  • Guidance language expanded to preliminary 2026 ranges, reflecting the transition into Phase III, compared to a focus on year-end cash and funding runway in Q3.
  • Strategic focus moved from late-stage data readouts to advancing into pivotal Phase III for REZPEG, with specific timelines for patient randomization and BLA submission targets.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from initial efficacy and safety signals to questions about competitive positioning, commercialization, and extension data.
  • Management’s confidence in the safety and efficacy of REZPEG remained high, while highlighting updated market opportunities and a narrowing competitive landscape.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the need to finalize 2026 budgeting for Phase III, with guidance ranges described as "very preliminary."
  • Potential competitive risks remain in the atopic dermatitis and alopecia areata spaces, but management emphasized REZPEG’s differentiated profile and the narrowing late-stage landscape.
  • The outcome of ongoing litigation with Eli Lilly was noted as an unresolved risk, with a trial scheduled for September.
  • Analysts questioned durability of response, efficacy benchmarks, and mitigation of injection site reactions, all of which management addressed with specific data points and planned monitoring strategies.

Final Takeaway

Nektar Therapeutics highlighted a transformative year marked by positive Phase IIb results for REZPEG, a strong cash infusion, and readiness for Phase III initiation in atopic dermatitis by June 2026. Management stressed the unique efficacy and safety profile of REZPEG, the potential for label expansion, and financial guidance projecting up to $460 million in year-end 2026 cash and investments, providing the resources to advance pivotal studies and target a 2029 BLA submission.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pono Capital Four prices $120M IPO at $10 per unit
data.description:
• Pono Capital Four (PONO), a Cayman Islands–based special purpose acquisition company, priced its $120M IPO of 12M units at $10.00 each.
  • The units are set to begin trading on Nasdaq under the ticker PONOU on March 13, 2026.
  • Each unit includes one Class A share and one right to receive one-fifth of a share after closing the business combination. Once separated, they are expected to trade on Nasdaq as PONO and PONOR, respectively.
  • The underwriters have a 45-day option to buy up to 1.8M additional units at the $10.00 IPO price, as the offering is expected to close March 16, 2026.

data.title: Mattr Non-GAAP EPS of C$0.04, revenue of C$312.5M; gives Q1 and FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Mattr press release (MTTRF): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of C$0.04.
  • Revenue of C$312.5M (+50.4% Y/Y).
  • Adjusted EBITDA2 was C$31.8 million, [+149.9% YoY].
  • Outlook: 
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of 2026 is expected to be similar to adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Revenue from Connection Technologies during 2026 is expected to be lower than 2025, primarily driven by expected reduced demand for wire and cable products in the Canadian industrial, Canadian mining, and global oilfield markets.
  • Revenue from Composite Technologies during 2026 is expected to be higher than 2025, as productive capacity continues to rise across the Xerxes network and the Flexpipe business gains incremental market share with larger diameter products.
  • The Company anticipates total full year 2026 capital expenditures to be C$35 to C$45 million.

data.title: VEON GAAP EPADS of -$0.44, revenue of $1.17B; gives FY26 outlook
data.description:
• VEON press release (VEON): Q4 GAAP EPADS of -$0.44.
  • Revenue of $1.17B (+17.2% Y/Y).
  • Digital Revenues increased 84.1% YoY in USD terms to USD 235 million and comprised 20.1% of total Group revenues in 4Q25, in part due to contributions from Uklon.
  • EBITDA for 4Q25 amounted to USD 527 million, representing a 29.1% YoY increase.
  • Group capex was USD 341 million in 4Q25.
  • For 2026, VEON expects total revenue growth of between 9% and 12% YoY in USD terms, and EBITDA growth of between 7% - 10% YoY in USD terms. VEON’s 2026 outlook for the Group’s capex intensity (excluding Ukraine) is in the range of 14%-16% for the full year 2026.

data.title: Open Lending outlines 2026 growth targets with 100,000–110,000 certified loans amid tightened underwriting and new platform launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Open Lending Corporation (LPRO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jessica Buss underscored a year of significant transformation, stating, "From day 1, my focus has been clear: stabilize the business and position it for durable growth. That meant improving profitability, reducing volatility in our profit share revenue, growing total revenue and customer retention, strengthening operational execution and building a culture of accountability." Buss highlighted the launch of the ApexOne Auto platform, which transitions Open Lending "from a single product company into a full spectrum decisioning and dynamic pricing engine." She emphasized the company's deliberate tightening of lending standards, explaining, "Our certified loan shortfall compared to guidance was driven by a temporary headwind in conversion rates as we actively managed risk and made targeted adjustments to how retail vehicle values were treated in our pricing models." Buss also announced the appointment of Anthony Capizzano as Chief Growth Officer, with priorities including increasing wallet share, penetrating larger institutions, and expanding the sales team.

  • CFO Massimo Monaco commented, "The business delivered stable financial performance as we continue to move beyond last year's change in estimate adjustment. Second, we made good progress on expense discipline while continuing to invest in key growth initiatives. And third, we strengthened the balance sheet through debt reduction and ongoing share repurchases."

Outlook

  • Open Lending provided full-year 2026 guidance of 100,000 to 110,000 certified loans and adjusted EBITDA of $25 million to $29 million. For the first quarter of 2026, certified loans are expected to be between 21,000 and 22,000. Buss stated, "We feel strongly that our conversion rate headwind from the fourth quarter has been completely solved at this point, and we believe we are well positioned for growth in '26." Management indicated that growth will compound through the year, with a heavier weighting toward the latter quarters. Buss added, "We believe introducing annual guidance for the first time since 2022 reflects our confidence in the growth trajectory of our business in 2026."

Financial Results

  • For the quarter, Open Lending facilitated 19,308 certified loans, generating revenue of $19.3 million and adjusted EBITDA of $2.8 million. The company reported a net income of $1.7 million and diluted net income per share of $0.01. Monaco noted, "Operating expenses were $13.9 million in the fourth quarter, compared to $15.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2024, representing a decrease of 9.3% year-over-year."

  • The business paid down approximately $50 million of its senior secured term loan and repurchased about 564,000 shares at an average price of $1.66 per share during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Madison Suhr, Raymond James: Asked about AI opportunities and risks. Buss responded, "We, as a technology company, obviously use many forms of AI in our tools and in our models...we believe that our models and what we've built, which are primarily using machine learning and the data that we have is far superior to what you could build with just a straight AI tool."

  • Suhr asked about the path from lower Q1 certified loan volume to projected full-year growth. Buss explained, "First quarter...contained a high number of credit builders and super thins, which we had, in essence, eliminated...Now the good news is that we do believe that incrementally quarter-over-quarter, we are going to experience the growth that we have in our cert projection...We have many tailwinds, I believe, to our growth story."

  • Joseph Vafi, Canaccord: Inquired about sequential certified loan growth and the health of the credit union channel. Buss replied, "OEM 1 and OEM 2 that remain stable and flat...We will see a ramp-up in OEM 3...the health of the credit unions is good. They've gotten more sophisticated. They are looking to grow."

  • Keaton Schuelke, Northland Securities: Asked about management buildout and credit quality. Buss stated, "At this point, all positions or all sort of senior executive positions that report to me and have accountability for running all aspects of our business are now filled." On delinquencies, Buss said, "the delinquencies that we're seeing on our most recent vintage are running about 200 basis points better at the 60-day delinquency mark than they were in vintage years '23 and '24."

  • Peter Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: Asked for guidance on free cash flow conversion. Buss responded, "we do not provide guidance on free cash flows" but noted, "we did collect profit share this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on AI, growth drivers, and management execution, suggesting a measured optimism about the company's trajectory.

  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone throughout, emphasizing disciplined risk management, strong platform positioning, and the successful execution of strategic initiatives. Buss reinforced confidence, stating, "We believe we are well positioned to build on that momentum in '26, while maintaining disciplined risk management."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive, moving from stabilization to growth, while analysts appeared more focused on growth catalysts and operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marked a shift from stabilization to deliberate growth, with the introduction of annual guidance for the first time since 2022. The launch of ApexOne Auto and the filling of all senior executive roles differentiate this quarter from the prior one.

  • Strategic focus evolved from tightening underwriting and stabilizing profit share volatility to expanding product offerings and driving certified loan growth. Key metrics such as certified loans and adjusted EBITDA were now attached to forward-looking targets.

  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on growth levers and management bench strength, while the previous quarter’s questions were weighted toward macro risks and product rollout.

  • Management’s confidence was more pronounced, bolstered by execution on cost controls and platform innovation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to temporary headwinds in conversion rates due to pricing adjustments as a challenge but indicated these were resolved by mid-January.

  • Buss highlighted the risk of overextension and mispricing in the auto lending industry, stating that Open Lending has "clearly chosen a different path" through tighter underwriting and disciplined pricing.

  • The company acknowledged ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties and the need to monitor delinquency trends, emphasizing that current vintages are outperforming recent years by 200 basis points at 60-day delinquency.

Final Takeaway

Open Lending’s management presented 2025 as a transformative year, underscored by disciplined risk management, cost control, and the launch of ApexOne Auto. With all senior leadership roles now filled and annual guidance set for 2026, the company aims for certified loan growth of 100,000 to 110,000 and adjusted EBITDA of $25 million to $29 million. The business is positioned to capitalize on platform expansion, improved customer retention, and opportunities in the credit union market, with a renewed focus on sustained, profitable growth across the credit spectrum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alibaba debuts OpenClaw app, intensifying China’s agentic AI race after Baidu’s DuClaw launch
data.description:
• Alibaba (BABA) launched a dedicated mobile app that it says allows users to install and deploy OpenClaw within minutes, intensifying competition among China’s tech giants to capitalize on the viral agentic AI assistant.
  • “JVS Claw” helps iOS and Android smartphone users without coding knowledge to instruct AI agents to perform simple real-world tasks, Bloomberg reported, citing Alibaba.
  • Free for 14 days, it emerged after Baidu (BIDU) released its own Android app DuClaw this week for OpenClaw, which helps users shop online and book travel, among other things. 
  • Baidu (BIDU) (BAIDF) is now offering a limited-time promotion in March to encourage experimentation among developers and AI enthusiasts. First-time users can subscribe for just RMB 17.8 per month (approximately $2.50 per month).
  • China’s enthusiasm for OpenClaw is fueling a rally in local technology stocks as companies quickly adopt the open-source artificial intelligence platform. Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) (TCTZF) shares rose as much as 6.2% Tuesday in Hong Kong after the internet giant launched WorkBuddy, an AI agent for workplace tasks that’s fully compatible with OpenClaw. Knowledge Atlas Technology JSC Ltd., known as Zhipu, surged as much as 16% after launching AutoClaw, a local version of the viral software. MiniMax Group Inc., which launched its agent earlier, had jumped 15%.

data.title: KinderCare outlines $2.7B–$2.75B revenue outlook for 2026 as enrollment challenges persist
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KinderCare Learning Companies, Inc. (KLC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Wyatt opened his first call since returning to lead KinderCare in December, noting, "Our recent performance has not been where we expected it to be, and that responsibility is ours." Wyatt emphasized the need for greater urgency and decisive action, describing initiatives to evolve operations and strengthen accountability. He highlighted KinderCare's recognition as one of Gallup's exceptional workplaces for the 10th consecutive year, crediting the dedication of educators and teams. Wyatt reported, "Including an extra week in the fourth quarter this year, revenue was $688 million, up 6% from last year. Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 was $68 million. Adjusted earnings per share was $0.12, and same center occupancy was 64.5%, down 340 basis points from last year."
  • Wyatt discussed progress in expanding the portfolio, including new center openings and acquisitions, with particular mention of entering Idaho and growing the Champions and Creme Schools brands. He stated, "Our newest brand, Creme Schools, contributed 4% to total revenue during the year. In 2025, we executed a focused reset of the brand, refined its positioning and strengthened operational and program consistency while opening 2 new schools."
  • Wyatt detailed a shift in management priorities, assigning Michael Canavin solely to KinderCare to drive enrollment, and announced changes in the short-term incentive plan to tie compensation to growth outcomes.
  • CFO Anthony Amandi stated, "Revenue was $688 million, up 6% year over year, primarily reflecting the incremental $45 million contribution from the 53rd week. On a comparable basis, revenue was essentially flat year over year." He noted a noncash goodwill impairment of $177 million, resulting in a Q4 net loss, but emphasized there was "no impact to our liquidity, debt covenants or ability to generate cash."

Outlook

  • Amandi provided 2026 guidance: "We expect revenue of $2.7 billion to $2.75 billion for the full year as compared to $2.69 billion on a 52-week basis in 2025." Adjusted EBITDA is expected at $210 million to $230 million, and adjusted EPS is projected at $0.10 to $0.20. The outlook assumes tuition-driven revenue growth of about 3%, offset by a 3% decline in same center occupancy. Free cash flow is projected between $35 million and $40 million, and CapEx is expected to run about 5% of revenue. For Q1 2026, revenue is anticipated in the range of $664 million to $674 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $45 million to $48 million and adjusted EPS about breakeven.
  • Management indicated, "Enrollment remains below prior year levels, and our outlook reflects the starting point. The actions taken last year and those underway now are intended to position the business to exit the year on a better trajectory."

Financial Results

  • KinderCare reported Q4 revenue of $688 million, benefiting from a 53rd week, and same center revenue of $618 million. Champions delivered $60 million in Q4 revenue, up 12% year-over-year. Creme Schools underperformed but is expected to improve with a refreshed curriculum and targeted initiatives.
  • The company reported a Q4 net loss of $177 million due to a noncash goodwill impairment. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $0.12. SG&A to revenue was 10.7%, down from the prior year. Interest expense declined significantly following debt repayment and repricing actions post-IPO.
  • For the full year, revenue reached $2.73 billion, with adjusted EBITDA just under $300 million. Same center occupancy declined to 67.8% for the year, with Q4 occupancy at 64.5%. Cash consideration for 26 acquisitions was $23 million, funded from $110 million in free cash flow.

Q&A

  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the drop in EBITDA margins and key priorities for improvement. CFO Amandi stated, "...the big call out is really all about those FTEs...as we have that lower occupancy expectation, that's the biggest thing that we're deleveraging." Wyatt outlined changes, including focusing Michael Canavin exclusively on KinderCare, clearing distractions for center directors, increased paid search investment, and making the incentive program 100% focused on growth.
  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: Inquired about M&A revenue and occupancy pacing. CFO Amandi reported, "Revenue from acquired centers was $6.2 million in the fourth quarter, totaling $14.9 million for the full year," and explained expected incremental growth through May, with inflection points during summer and back-to-school periods. Wyatt added optimism for breaking negative trends.
  • Jeffrey Meuler, Baird: Asked about industry structural health and consistency for families. Wyatt stated, "...retention...has been stable and actually grown a tiny bit." He attributed underperformance to distractions for center directors, not retention.
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays: Asked about government funding uncertainty and portfolio actions. Wyatt said, "We don't see any interruptions to it [block grant/subsidy]...we have a staff of a few hundred that administer the subsidy disbursements in our company...a competitive advantage." Amandi noted an ongoing review of all centers, suggesting closures above the usual 15-20 may occur.
  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: Probed the enrollment decline. Wyatt indicated much is "self-inflicted," with distractions removed and best practices from high-performing regions being scaled network-wide.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on margin declines, enrollment challenges, government funding risks, and the effectiveness of operational changes. The tone was slightly negative, emphasizing skepticism and concern over persistent issues and the pace of recovery.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone in prepared remarks but was more defensive in the Q&A, repeatedly referencing past successes and expressing confidence in new strategies. Wyatt said, "...we're being conservative...but I'll be very disappointed if we stay with that."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism and management defensiveness increased, with more direct questioning of the structural versus cyclical nature of enrollment declines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is lower, with anticipated declines in adjusted EBITDA and EPS despite continued revenue growth, compared to a more stable outlook in Q3 2025.
  • Strategic focus shifted more directly to urgent operational improvements, decisive portfolio actions, and tying incentives to growth. The previous quarter highlighted digital tools and leadership promotions, while the current quarter stresses leadership concentration and aggressive marketing.
  • Analysts increasingly focused on structural industry challenges, state subsidy risks, and the effectiveness of management's turnaround efforts. Management's tone is more urgent and acknowledges deeper internal issues versus the previous quarter's emphasis on external headwinds.
  • Occupancy and enrollment trends remain negative, and management is less certain about a near-term return to the historical growth algorithm.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistent enrollment declines and underutilization of centers are key concerns, with same center occupancy down year-over-year.
  • The risk of further center closures is heightened as management reviews the portfolio, with the potential for more closures than the usual 15-20.
  • Confusion and uncertainty around federal and state childcare subsidies continue but are being actively managed through government engagement.
  • Margin pressure expected due to lower occupancy, reduced grants, and increased marketing investments.

Final Takeaway

KinderCare's leadership outlined a renewed focus on operational discipline, urgent action to boost enrollment, and decisive management changes to address recent underperformance. Despite macroeconomic and policy uncertainties, the company set clear 2026 financial targets and signaled that results will take time as new strategies are implemented. Management emphasized its commitment to stabilizing occupancy, improving underperforming centers, and maintaining financial discipline while navigating a challenging environment for the childcare sector.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Honda shares plunge as $15.7B EV write-down triggers first annual loss since 1957
Allogene targets pivotal ALPHA3 data in April and extends cash runway into 2028 while prioritizing scalable CAR T programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Allogene Therapeutics (ALLO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Chang described 2026 as a “defining year” focused on “delivering meaningful clinical milestones with rigor and speed.” He emphasized that Allogene’s approach is to “validate our allogeneic platform, not nearly as an alternative, but as the imperative path to making cell therapy scalable, accessible and deliverable at biologic-like scale.”
  • The company’s main clinical priorities are the ALPHA3 study of Cema-Cel in first-line large B-cell lymphoma and the ALLO-329 program for autoimmune disease, with key proof points expected in April and June respectively. Chang stated the ALPHA3 goal is about “shifting the paradigm in the first-line treatment and demonstrating that Cema-Cel can reduce the risk of relapse and improve the cure rate.”
  • Allogene is now running over 60 active clinical sites across the U.S. and Canada, with expansion underway in Australia and South Korea, indicating a broadened clinical footprint.
  • Chang reported that the company’s cash runway has been extended into the first quarter of 2028, enabling focused execution on the prioritized pipeline.
  • CFO Geoffrey Parker stated, “As of December 31, 2025, we had $258.3 million in cash, cash equivalents and investments,” and highlighted the receipt of $23.7 million from an escrow related to arbitration, as well as $20.7 million raised via the ATM equity facility. Parker noted, “We have extended our cash runway into the first quarter of 2028, which we believe covers the time frame we currently estimate is needed to complete enrollment in the ALPHA3 trial.”
  • Parker added, “Net loss for the fourth quarter was $38.8 million or $0.17 per share, including noncash stock-based compensation expense of $8.1 million.”

Outlook

  • Allogene is guiding for operating cash expense in 2026 to be approximately $150 million, with GAAP operating expenses expected to be approximately $210 million, including about $35 million in noncash stock-based compensation.
  • Management expects pivotal data from ALPHA3 in April and proof-of-concept data for ALLO-329 in June. Parker said, “With pivotal data from ALPHA3 approaching in April, proof-of-concept data for ALLO-329 expected in June and cash runway now extended into 2028, we believe we are well capitalized to execute through these important inflection points.”

Financial Results

  • As of December 31, 2025, Allogene reported $258.3 million in cash, cash equivalents and investments.
  • Fourth quarter R&D expenses were $28.6 million, including $2.5 million of noncash stock-based compensation. G&A expenses for Q4 were $13.8 million, including $5.6 million in noncash stock-based compensation.
  • Net loss for Q4 was $38.8 million or $0.17 per share. Full year 2025 net loss was $190.9 million or $0.87 per share.
  • The company received $23.7 million from a favorable arbitration outcome and raised an additional $20.7 million year-to-date.

Q&A

  • Tyler Van Buren, TD Cowen: Asked about safety parameters for ALPHA3 and the bar for broad uptake in the community. Zachary Roberts responded, “We plan to provide some high-level safety information enough for everybody to understand how well this is being tolerated... this needs to be a therapy that can be delivered as an outpatient. It does not lead to rehospitalization due to adverse events.”
  • Biren Amin, Piper Sandler: Inquired about the expected MRD difference and interim EFS analysis. Roberts said, “We do believe that 25% to 30% is going to translate to very meaningful clinical difference in the outcome... we are constantly reviewing the data and modifying our assumptions.”
  • Michael Yee, UBS: Asked about the relationship between MRD clearance and EFS and the target product profile for the autoimmune program. Chang stated, “There isn’t enough data out there to see how MRD clearance, the relationship between that and clinical outcomes such as event-free survival... our focus right now is the MRD clearance that we promised to communicate in April of this year.” He added regarding ALLO-329, “We are testing as part of the study, no lymphodepletion. So target product profile... is providing a meaningful B-cell depletion that’s leading to reset of the immune system.”
  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Cema-Cel’s positioning as CD3 bispecifics move forward. Roberts said, “We believe that the MRD positivity rate is largely going to be unchanged for the next many years.”
  • John Newman, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about data on depletion of CD19 and CD70 cells for ALLO-329. Chang responded, “At the time of the data analysis, many of the things and more, a lot of translational data, we have plans... the data communication... will be the focus of the data communication.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on safety, MRD endpoints, and commercial positioning, with repeated inquiries about the impact of changing standards in lymphoma and autoimmune disease.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and financial stability. During Q&A, management responses were measured, and at times, cautious, particularly regarding statistical and interim analysis details. Chang stated, “Let’s stay tuned. I mean our focus right now is the MRD clearance that we promised to communicate in April of this year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a focus on data-driven execution, but management’s tone shifted from emphasizing conviction and platform vision to prioritizing imminent clinical proof points and disciplined resource allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced more precise timelines for ALPHA3 and ALLO-329 data releases, compared to the previous quarter’s broader emphasis on platform conviction.
  • Guidance now extends cash runway into Q1 2028, longer than the previous quarter’s estimate of runway into the second half of 2027.
  • Management narrowed operational focus to ALPHA3 and ALLO-329, while in the prior quarter, the clinical pipeline including Traverse was highlighted.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from general trial progress and enrollment to more technical questions regarding MRD endpoints, site readiness, and the impact of evolving standards in lymphoma treatment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the need for “disciplined execution” and “prioritization” as the company cannot advance all programs at once.
  • There are operational challenges associated with expanding into community settings and new geographic regions.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the relationship between MRD clearance and clinical outcomes, as well as the potential effects of new therapies on patient eligibility and market size.
  • Management highlighted that achieving targeted MRD differentials is crucial for trial success and future market positioning.

Final Takeaway

Allogene Therapeutics is entering a pivotal year marked by upcoming clinical catalysts: interim ALPHA3 data in April and ALLO-329 proof-of-concept in June. Management has extended the company’s cash runway into early 2028, sharpened strategic focus on high-priority programs, and reaffirmed commitment to evidence-driven execution. With multiple data readouts on the horizon and resources aligned to support these milestones, the company is positioned to validate its allogeneic CAR T platform and pursue scalable, accessible therapies for oncology and autoimmune disease.
